text: The liver is retracted using a gauze suture, while near-infrared (NIR) imaging is employed to visualize the tumor injected with indocyanine green. NIR imaging also highlights the draining lymph nodes during the procedure.

The surgeon performs a partial omentectomy, initiating dissection four centimeters from the greater curvature of the stomach towards the spleen. The upper border of the pancreas is dissected to facilitate access to station eleven lymph nodes.

The surgeon continues dissection up to the short gastric vessels, targeting the left gastroepiploic vessels at their roots. The gastrosplenic ligament is separated up to the left side of the esophageal hiatus by dividing the short gastric vessels from the spleen's surface to access and dissect the four SA lymph nodes.
The surgeon uses near-infrared vision (NIR) to visualize and thoroughly clear station eleven D and ten lymph nodes at the splenic hilum, ensuring no remaining lymph nodes are visible.

The surgeon continues the division of the gastric colic ligament distally towards the pylorus. The right gastroepiploic vessels are identified and divided at their roots. A swab is placed behind the duodenum, visible during anterior caucarization.

The surgeon transects the duodenum one to two centimeters distal to the pylorus using a linear stapler inserted by the assistant. Subsequently, the lesser omentum is opened and divided up to the right side of the esophageal hiatus.

The surgeon exposes and divides the right gastric artery at its origin. Soft tissues surrounding the proper hepatic artery and the medial side of the portal vein are cleared to facilitate the dissection of lymph node stations VIIIa and XIIa.
The surgeon exposes and divides the left gastric vein at the upper margin of the common hepatic artery, followed by the exposure and division of the left gastric artery at its origin.

The surgeon detaches the retroperitoneal attachment of the stomach up to the right crus, mobilizes the abdominal esophagus, and divides the vagus nerves. The distal esophagus is transected using a linear stapler, and the specimen is placed in a bag.

The surgeon delivers a loop of proximal jejunum towards the esophagus and performs an anticholic esophagojejunostomy using a linear stapler. The enterotomy is then stapled closed, completing the anastomosis.
The surgeon creates an enterotomy in the jejunum, fifty centimeters distal to the esophagojejunostomy anastomosis, in preparation for the jejuno-jejunostomy.
The surgeon disconnects the biliropancreatic limb from the OJ anastomosis and positions it for the stapled jejuno-jejunostomy (JJ anastomosis). The enterotomy is closed using a stapler.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defects and places a drain in situ. A four-centimeter incision is made to retrieve the surgical specimen.
